factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies market using ANZSIC C2221 or ANZSIC06 C2221 classification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses the structural steel fabrication market but never identifies it using the ANZSIC C2221 or ANZSIC06 C2221 classification code. No ANZSIC code is mentioned anywhere in the market definition, data sections, or sources list. Therefore the required classification identification is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States employment figures in range 4,200-4,600 for multiple years within 2018-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives employment figures of about 4,100 (for 2025) and about 5,000 (general/undated), but it does not state employment values within 4,200–4,600 for multiple specific years between 2018 and 2024. It also lacks a year-by-year employment series for 2018–2024 in that range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States market revenue approximately NZD 1.4-1.9 billion for 2023-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the sector’s revenue was “estimated at around NZD 1.8 billion” (albeit by 2025) and thus includes a market revenue figure within the NZD 1.4–1.9 billion range for the 2023–2024 timeframe context.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States domestic fabrication capacity between 120,000-150,000 tonnes per annum
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that annual fabrication capacity expanded from about 120,000 tonnes in 2018 to an estimated 150,000 tonnes by 2023, which falls within the 120,000–150,000 tonnes per annum range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides geographic locations for at least 3 major fabricators (e.g., Auckland, Christchurch, Whangārei, Lower Hutt, Timaru)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists several major fabricators but does not provide geographic locations (e.g., city/region) for at least three of them. It mentions Steel & Tube has “27 locations nationwide” and notes Auckland/Cantabury/Waikato industry clusters, but it does not tie specific locations to individual fabricators like Culham Engineering, D&H Steel, or Pacific Steel.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies HS codes 7207-7216 or relevant subset for structural steel imports
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly references the HS code range “7207 to 7216” in the import tariff section when discussing raw steel tariffs, satisfying the requirement to specify those HS codes or a relevant subset.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States most structural steel inputs under HS 7207-7216 are duty-free under NZ Normal Tariff, while noting some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5% rates
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses HS 7207–7216 tariffs only in general terms (e.g., “0%, 5%, or 10%”) and does not state that most structural steel inputs under HS 7207–7216 are duty-free under the NZ Normal Tariff. It also fails to specifically note that some tariff lines—such as certain HS 7216 sections—carry 5% rates.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link steel demand: approximately 30,000-32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000-5,000 tonnes structural
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives City Rail Link steel demand as “approximately 25,000 tonnes of reinforcing and structural steel,” which does not match the required ranges of 30,000–32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000–5,000 tonnes structural. It also does not separately state reinforcing vs structural tonnages within those specified bands.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link completion or opening circa 2026
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the City Rail Link is “scheduled for completion around 2024–2026,” which includes a circa-2026 completion/opening timeframe.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel approximately 9,000 tonnes
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not mention Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel or provide an approximate figure of 9,000 tonnes. It discusses other projects (e.g., City Rail Link) and general market tonnage, but contains no statement about Te Kaha’s roof steel quantity.

The response discusses several infrastructure projects and timelines but never mentions Te Kaha Stadium or states that it opens in 2026. There is no reference to Te Kaha Stadium anywhere in the text, so the required opening year statement is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies New Dunedin Hospital as major infrastructure project requiring significant structural steel components
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists major infrastructure projects such as City Rail Link, East West Link, and Takitimu North Link, but it never mentions New Dunedin Hospital or identifies it as a major project requiring significant structural steel components.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Reconciles conflicting market size figures by explaining scope definition differences (narrow product vs. broad service boundaries)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response presents multiple market size figures (e.g., NZD 1.8b vs NZD 78m) but does not explicitly reconcile them by explaining differing scope definitions such as narrow product versus broad service boundaries. It lacks a clear discussion of why the figures conflict and what each figure includes/excludes. No scope-based reconciliation is provided.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes construction fabricators from distributors/mills/producers when identifying top companies (e.g., Steel & Tube, Pacific Steel)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response lists “Steel & Tube” and “New Zealand Steel” among the top fabricators while describing them as distribution/processing and an integrated steel producer/mill, respectively, rather than clearly separating them from true construction fabricators. It also includes “Pacific Steel” primarily as a reinforcing supplier, not explicitly as a structural fabricator. This fails to distinguish fabricators from distributors/mills/producers in the top-company identification.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains why low/zero tariffs remove price umbrella, making margins dependent on global mill prices/FX/freight rather than border protection
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response notes that NZ tariffs are low/modest and that global steel price volatility and freight costs affect economics, but it does not explain the specific “price umbrella” mechanism whereby low/zero tariffs remove border protection and make margins primarily driven by global mill prices, FX, and freight.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies actionable method to obtain class-level data (e.g., filter Stats NZ AES CSV by ANZSIC L4 code for C2221)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not provide an actionable, step-by-step method for obtaining class-level data from Stats NZ/MBIE (e.g., downloading AES CSV and filtering by ANZSIC L4 code such as C2221). It cites various sources but never explains how to extract or filter official datasets to isolate the relevant class-level series.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects employment or revenue trends to construction cycle timing (e.g., 2023 peak preceding mid-2022 slowdown)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses cyclical influences like early-2020s decline and post-COVID volatility, but it does not explicitly tie employment or revenue movements to specific construction-cycle timing (e.g., a 2023 peak following a mid-2022 slowdown). There is no clear temporal linkage between trend inflection points and the construction cycle.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Validates stated fabrication capacity/throughput by aggregating steel demand across multiple named projects with tonnages
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response lists steel demand for individual projects (e.g., CRL at ~25,000 tonnes) but does not aggregate steel demand across multiple named projects to validate any stated fabrication capacity/throughput. It also does not explicitly compare the summed project tonnage against the claimed industry capacity figures (e.g., 150,000 t).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies absent official tonnage series and proposes specific triangulation approach using SCNZ capacity, trade data, or project disclosures
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides tonnage/capacity estimates from SCNZ and discusses fabrication volume, but it does not explicitly identify that an official Stats NZ/MBIE tonnage series is absent nor does it propose a concrete triangulation method (e.g., step-by-step using SCNZ capacity plus trade HS 7207–7216 imports/exports or project steel take-offs).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Attributes fabricator margin pressure or competitive challenges to NZ import tariffs or border protection rather than global price/FX exposure
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly frames margin pressure and key risks around NZ import tariffs (e.g., “Tariff Exposure” and “tariffs…squeezing profit margins”) rather than primarily attributing challenges to global steel price and FX exposure. While it briefly mentions international price volatility, the emphasis is on tariffs as a notable driver of margin pressure.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents all quantitative data with explicit units (NZD, tonnes, %), specific timeframes (years/FY), and measurement precision
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes many figures with units like NZD, tonnes, km, and % (e.g., “NZD 5.493 billion,” “25,000 tonnes,” “3.45 km,” “-6.0%”). However, it does not present all quantitative data with specific year/FY timeframes (many are “by 2025,” “around 2024–2026,” or “estimated”) and lacks consistent precision across the 2018–2024 series.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis into distinct sections addressing each requested dimension (market size, companies, tariffs, projects, risks)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is clearly divided into separate sections for market size/growth, top companies, tariffs, major projects, and strategic risks/opportunities, matching each requested dimension.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional market research tone without promotional language, speculation, or unsupported forward claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes speculation and unsupported forward-looking claims, such as multiple “forecasts/projections” (e.g., projected CAGRs, recovery outlook) and generalized statements about being “well-positioned,” without tying them to Stats NZ/MBIE evidence. This violates the requirement to avoid speculation and unsupported forward claims, even though the tone is mostly professional.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents historical employment, revenue, or capacity data (2018-2024) in tables or structured year-by-year format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response provides some historical figures (e.g., capacity in 2018 and 2023) but does not present employment, revenue, or capacity data for 2018–2024 in a table or structured year-by-year format. It mainly gives single-year estimates and narrative trends rather than a complete 2018–2024 series.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquial phrasing, rhetorical questions, or informal register inappropriate for investment-grade research
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, report-style tone with structured headings and bullet points. It does not use rhetorical questions or notably colloquial/informal phrasing inappropriate for investment-grade research.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses excessive wordiness, filler phrases, or unnecessary elaboration that extends length without adding substantive information
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is lengthy and includes several broad, generic narrative sections (e.g., “Market dynamics are influenced by external factors…”, “Broader Effects…”, and a multi-paragraph conclusion) that add little new data. It also contains repeated contextual elaboration and qualifiers rather than sticking tightly to the requested Stats NZ/MBIE historical tables and specific tariff/HS-code details.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Business Demography or equivalent Stats NZ publication for employment figures
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites Steel Construction New Zealand, IBISWorld, MBIE, and other sources, but it does not cite Stats NZ Business Demography or any equivalent Stats NZ publication for the employment figures. No Stats NZ employment dataset/publication link or date is provided for those employment numbers.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or equivalent Stats NZ economic survey for revenue/sales data
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites Steel Construction New Zealand, IBISWorld, MBIE, and other sources, but it does not cite Stats NZ’s Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or any equivalent Stats NZ economic survey as the source for revenue/sales data.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NZ Customs Working Tariff Document or official NZ tariff schedule for HS code duty rates
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites New Zealand Customs’ official “Tariff classifications and rates” page and references a “customs tariff schedule,” which constitutes an official NZ tariff schedule source for HS duty rates.

citation-quality.requirement: References Steel Construction NZ (SCNZ) or industry body for fabrication capacity/throughput data
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites Steel Construction New Zealand (SCNZ) industry updates and uses them as sources for fabrication capacity/throughput figures such as annual fabrication volume and spare capacity.

citation-quality.requirement: Either references NZ Companies Office as a data source OR notes that private fabricators have limited public financials
citation-quality.explanation: The response claims that the top fabricators were identified using “Companies Office filings” and references “Companies Office filings, annual reports” in the Top 5 section, which constitutes referencing NZ Companies Office as a data source.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites specific data sources for at least 2 major infrastructure projects (CRL, Te Kaha, or hospital projects)
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites specific sources for City Rail Link (e.g., New Zealand Steel “Local Steel for Auckland’s City Rail Link” and City Rail Link updates) but does not cite any sources for Te Kaha or hospital projects, and the other projects listed (East West Link, Takitimu North Link) lack specific steel-demand source citations. Therefore it does not provide at least two major projects (CRL, Te Kaha, or hospital projects) with specific data sources.
